HYEGLO Beyond Awareness

A Creative Strategy Perspective

Prepared by  ·  Arshia Gupta  ·  Creative Strategist & Content Writer

Scroll
01

Editor's Letter

Dear Kanupriya,
This is not an audit.

A personal letter · From Arshia

I want to be honest with you from the first line. This is not a social media report. It is not a content calendar. It is not a competitor analysis dressed up in pink. What you're about to read is an exploration — a thoughtful examination of what the next chapter of Hyeglo could look like, written by someone who spent real time understanding the brand before saying a word about it.

I read your founder communication carefully. I watched the way you explain skinified makeup — not just what it is, but why it needed to exist. I noticed the visual language, the pink, the laboratory imagery, the way you hold both science and emotion in the same frame.

"Hyeglo has already done the hardest part. It has made people understand. The next question is whether it can make people belong."

Most early-stage beauty brands are fighting to be seen. They pour energy into awareness — explaining who they are, what they make, why it matters. You have largely moved past that. People know the founder. People understand what skinified makeup means. The why is visible.

What I noticed is a different kind of opportunity entirely. Not about more content. Not about louder marketing. About creating a world around the product that people actually want to inhabit.

Arshia
Arshia Gupta  ·  Creative Strategist & Content Writer

What Hyeglo Has Already Built

The foundation
is already here.

Before we talk about what's next, it's worth pausing to recognize what's in place. Because Hyeglo hasn't been building content — it's been building trust. And that is a much harder thing to do.

01
✦

Founder Visibility

Kanupriya shows up with intention — explaining, demonstrating, and standing behind the product with genuine conviction. That level of founder presence is rare and incredibly valuable.

02
◈

Product Storytelling

The "skinified makeup" positioning isn't just clever — it's educational. Hyeglo has communicated a complex philosophy in a way that feels accessible without being dumbed down.

03
◇

Ingredient Education

Scientific credibility has been translated into language that resonates emotionally. That intersection of knowledge and feeling is rare in the D2C beauty space.

04
○

Brand Purpose

Better makeup, built for skin. It's a promise that's both functional and emotional — which is exactly where the best beauty brands live.

05
◉

Visual Consistency

The pink-forward identity, the laboratory visual language, the editorial quality of the content — Hyeglo looks like a brand. It feels cohesive in a way many early-stage brands never achieve.

06
✧

Early Trust Building

Awards. Maker kit placements. Community recognition. The seeds of social proof are already in the soil. The question now is how to help them compound.

PARTICIPATION

The Inflection Point

When awareness stops
being the problem.

Phase One

Getting Known

Most early-stage brands struggle here. The work is relentless: explaining who you are, what you make, why it matters. Fighting for attention in a crowded feed.

→

Where Hyeglo Is Now

Creating Belonging

People already know the founder. People already understand the product. The next question isn't "how do we get seen?" — it's "how do we make people want to stay?"

"The challenge is no longer awareness. The challenge is participation — inviting people not just to buy, but to belong. To see themselves in the brand. To make it part of how they talk about beauty."

Hyeglo Visual Identity

Two Kinds of Beauty Brands

Beauty brands people buy
vs. brands people join.

The Transaction Layer

Brands people purchase.

  • The relationship begins and ends at the product
  • Repurchase depends on formulation, price, availability
  • No emotional stickiness beyond the product experience
  • Susceptible to comparison and switching

The Identity Layer

Brands people belong to.

  • The brand becomes part of how they see themselves
  • Customers advocate without being asked
  • Community creates content, conversation, and loyalty
  • Identity, ritual, and belonging drive repurchase
Rhode Rare Beauty Glossier

Ingredient Storytelling

From ingredient
to experience.

Rhode's food-first sensory campaigns taught the beauty world something important: the best way to explain what a product feels like is not to describe the ingredient — it's to evoke the sensation.

Cherry glaze. Glazed donut skin. Peptide lip treatment. These aren't just names — they're entire sensory worlds that make a product feel desirable before a single application.

Cocoa Butter — not a moisturiser. A warm, enveloping richness. The skin equivalent of velvet.
Tin Peptides (TINTELLIGENCE) — not a formula. A conversation between skin and makeup. Intelligent adaptation.
The Finish — not coverage. The feeling of your skin, but on its best morning ever.

The Rhode playbook: make ingredients delicious

Food Sensory Direction

Products as ritual objects, not just formulas

Ritual Storytelling

The end result is always a feeling, not a finish

Skin Result Direction

The Opportunity

Building the world
around the product.

A beauty brand is rarely built through content alone. It's built at the intersection of eight forces working together — and Hyeglo has the foundation to activate all of them.

What Participation Could Look Like

Six ways to make
people feel seen.

Participation isn't a marketing tactic. It's an invitation — to show up, to share, to see yourself reflected in the brand.

01

Customer Stories

The everyday beauty diary.

Real customers. Real mornings. Real rituals. Intimate, honest glimpses into the moments where Hyeglo becomes part of the day.

02

Beauty Rituals

The skincare-makeup ritual.

Hyeglo sits at the intersection of skin and makeup. The ritual of applying it — the texture, the finish — is a story worth telling again and again.

03

Founder Conversations

Behind the formula.

The behind-the-scenes conversations — about formulation choices, about what didn't work — build depth that no campaign can replicate.

04

Community Features

Making customers the story.

Featured customer moments, shared beauty philosophies. When customers see themselves in the brand, they don't just buy — they advocate.

05

Confidence Stories

Before & after the mirror.

Not transformation content. Confidence content. The subtle shift when you find makeup that works with your skin rather than against it.

06

User Diaries

Thirty days with Hyeglo.

Long-form customer narratives. The experience of living with the product. The kind of content that makes strangers feel like they already know the brand.

The Pink Notes
Content Territory No. 01 A Hyeglo Editorial Column

Column Concept

The Pink Notes

A recurring editorial column at the intersection of beauty ritual, skin education, and personal expression. Not a tutorial. Not a campaign. A conversation that readers return to — because it feels like it was written for them specifically.

"Not every morning needs a full face. But every morning deserves skin that feels taken care of. That's where we start." — The Pink Notes, Issue 01

Reel Concept

The 4-minute ritual

A soft, ASMR-adjacent morning ritual reel. No talking. Just sound, texture, and the quiet beauty of skinified makeup meeting skin. The kind of content people save and rewatch because it makes them feel calm.

Warm lighting · product sounds · linen textures · natural finish reveal

Carousel Concept

What your skin is saying

A 6-slide carousel interpreting skin signals — redness, texture, dryness — and showing how skinified makeup responds rather than hides. Educational. Personal. Deeply shareable.

Voice & Tone

How it sounds

Warm. Intelligent. Slightly poetic. Never clinical. The Pink Notes speaks like a friend who happens to know everything about skin.

"Built for days when your skin is doing the most. And days when it isn't. Either way, you're covered."

Visual Direction

The aesthetic language

Soft pink gradients. Close-up texture. Morning light through curtains. Skin in its natural state, then with Hyeglo. Editorial spacing. Hand-held intimacy. Feels like a love letter to good skin days.

Creator & Community Ecosystem

The people who
make it real.

Micro-creators don't amplify brands. They authenticate them. When the right person shares Hyeglo organically, it doesn't feel like advertising — it feels like a recommendation from a trusted friend.

✦
Identify
Skin-first creators who already speak the skinified language.
◈
Brief
A creative brief that feels like an invitation, not a script.
◇
Create
UGC that looks native. Honest product experiences. Real skin stories.
○
Amplify
The best community content gets celebrated and turned into paid creative.

Tier One

The Community Voice

1K — 10K

Micro-creators with high engagement. Their recommendation carries weight precisely because they aren't famous. Product gifting. Story features. Community spotlight.

Tier Two

The Beauty Educator

10K — 100K

Skin-focused creators who understand formulation and can speak to skinified makeup with credibility. Long-form reviews. Before & after documentation.

Tier Three

The Cultural Amplifier

100K+

Selective partnerships with larger creators whose aesthetic and audience align perfectly. Not for reach alone — for cultural signal.

HYEGLO

Why I Am Reaching Out

I don't think Hyeglo needsanother content writer.

I believe Hyeglo has reached a stage where content, community, creators, positioning, and participation need to work together. Not as separate workstreams. As a single, coherent strategy.

That's the work I enjoy doing. Working at the intersection of storytelling, audience psychology, positioning, and brand building.

I'm a freelance Creative Strategist and Content Writer. If this direction resonates with how you see the future of Hyeglo, I'd love to have a conversation.

Thank you.
